title: Day 02 Quiz — Props, State, and Controlled Forms
description: Verify you understand objects/spread/rest, TypeScript props, useState, controlled inputs, and event handling before Day 03.

questions.prompt: What is the primary difference between props and state?

options.text: Props are mutable; state is read-only
Props are passed from parent; state is owned and updated inside the component
Props use useState; state uses useEffect
There is no difference in functional components

questions.explanation: Props configure a component from outside and should be treated as read-only by the child. State is internal data that changes over time via setters.

questions.prompt: In a controlled input, the DOM holds the authoritative value and React reads it on submit.
questions.explanation: Controlled inputs store the value in React state. The input's value prop is driven by state; onChange updates state on every keystroke.
Rest collects remaining properties into contact. role is removed; email remains on contact.

questions.prompt: Why should you call event.preventDefault() in a form submit handler?

options.text: To prevent React from re-rendering
To stop the browser's default full-page form submission and reload
To enable TypeScript strict mode
To clear useState automatically

questions.explanation: Without preventDefault, the browser performs a native submit and reloads the page, destroying the SPA experience.

questions.prompt: Which are valid ways to update form state immutably? Select all that apply.

options.text: setForm((prev) => ({ ...prev, email: value }))
form.email = value; setForm(form)
setForm({ ...form, email: value }) when form is current state from the same render
Object.assign({}, form, { email: value }) then setForm(result)

questions.explanation: Option b mutates the existing object before setState — React may bail out. Functional updates (a) are safest when depending on prior state. (c) works when form is fresh from current render. (d) creates a new object correctly.

questions.prompt: A controlled input pairs {{blank1}}={state} with {{blank2}} that updates that state.
questions.explanation: value + onChange is the controlled component contract in React.

questions.prompt: Complete the hook that stores a string password field.
questions.explanation: useState returns a tuple: current value and setter function named by convention set + PascalCase field name.

questions.prompt: What does one-way data flow mean in React forms?

options.text: Data only flows from child to parent
Props flow down; events/callbacks flow up with new data
State is stored only in the root App component
Inputs cannot use onChange

questions.explanation: Parents pass props down. Children notify parents via callbacks. State may live in child or parent depending on design, but the flow direction is consistent.

questions.prompt: Why is mutating state directly (e.g. form.email = 'x') dangerous with useState?
questions.sampleAnswer: useState compares object references. Mutating the existing object does not create a new reference, so React may not re-render. It also breaks time-travel debugging and predictable updates.
questions.explanation: Interviewers use this to probe immutability understanding before Redux and complex state.

questions.prompt: An input appears frozen while typing. value={email} is set but onChange is missing. What happens?

options.text: React throws an error immediately
The input is read-only from React's perspective — keystrokes cannot update displayed value
useState resets to empty string
The browser ignores value prop

questions.explanation: Without onChange, state never updates, so value stays fixed. This is a classic controlled component mistake.
